headlines.title: Bay Area shoppers concerned following cyclosporiasis outbreak
headlines.description: Many shoppers are now paying closer attention to where their food comes from as Taylor Farms removed iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico following an FDA investigation linking some of its lettuce to a cyclosporiasis outbreak. Shoppers at the Mountain Farmers Market in Los Gatos said they prefer buying directly from the people who grow their produce. “I love to meet the farmers, and I feel like just knowing where your food comes from is amazing. It just feels really good. It’s beautiful produce,” Los Gatos resident Mary Curtiss said. The concern follows an FDA investigation linking shredded iceberg lettuce served at taco bell restaurants in Indiana, Kentucky, Michigan, Ohio, and west Virginia to a cyclosporiasis outbreak. Recalls 18 hours ago Health Jul 16 Health & Wellness Jul 15 Friday, Taco Bell released a statement after some of its customers reported feeling ill saying it would remove all Taylor Farms affected lettuce from its products. Cyclosporiasis is a parasite that can contaminate fresh produce, causing severe and prolonged diarrhea. The FDA urged customers in those states to avoid shredded iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms Mexico. Taylor Farms says the FDA traceback points to a single independent farm, representing less than 1% of the nation’s iceberg lettuce supply. But as a precaution, the company says it’s removing all iceberg lettuce from that growing region indefinitely. The FDA says 1,644 people infected with cyclosporiasis who reported eating at taco bell have been reported by the five affected states. Meanwhile, the CDC says it has received reports of 1,645 confirmed domestic cases of cyclosporiasis since May 1, with more than 5,100 additional reports still under review to determine whether they are domestically acquired cases of cyclosporiasis. In California, health officials say there are no known local outbreaks. “The fact that we haven’t seen that happen in California while so much is going in the Midwest, suggests that our food distribution, or the lot of vegetables that was implicated in the Midwest, was not sent to California, because you would expect a lot more people to be sick,” UCSF Infectious Disease Specialist Dr. Peter Chin-Hong said. While California isn’t seeing a spike in cases, Chin-Hong says there are still simple ways to reduce risk. “Use fruits that has a peel like avocados, melons, mangos, oranges, bananas, things you can peel. Because the inside of the fruit generally has very little contact with soil,” he said. For those buying lettuce, Chin-Hong recommends choosing a whole head instead of bagged greens whenever possible and removing the outer leaves before eating. Health officials recommended those who develop severe or prolonged diarrhea to contact their healthcare provider.

headlines.title: South Bay authorities respond to Trump's voter fraud allegations
headlines.description: The Department of Homeland Security is demanding that California and several other states respond to allegations of widespread voter registration problems involving noncitizens. President Donald Trump leveled the charges during an address from the White House on Thursday. This also comes as Trump is trying to the Save America Act, which would require everyone to provide proof of citizenship in order to register to vote. On Friday, Trump sent the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) after states like California, where they claim close to a quarter of a million non-citizens are on the state’s voter rolls, without saying how they came up with that number. And while a federal judge recently found that DHS wrongly classified a number of actual us citizens who had registered to be non-citizens, Matt Moreles with the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters said “When this has come up, it’s been really isolated.” DHS says state election officials have to run their voter records through a federal database looking for non-citizens, or face severe penalties, including jail time. “The conversation around are noncitizens registering, are noncitizens voting has been going on for years now, and we still haven’t seen any tangible credible evidence that this is a widespread problem,” Moreles said. According to the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters, between 90 and 95% of their ballots are now sent by mail. And despite voters across the country, including at times Trump himself, voting successfully by mail, the president also called out that system as fraudulent. “Hopefully there will be no corrupt mail in ballots, which if you watched California and so many other places, mail-in ballots are inherently corrupt,” he said. A charge the county registrar has heard before, but so far with no corruption found. “We have an extremely secure vote by mail process, extremely successful, I think we have a proven track record here in our county,” Merelos said. In response to the accusations, Governor Gavin Newsom spoke out on Friday, going on social media to deny any voter fraud in California and saying Trump’s goal of making people show ID when they vote will help the Republican party. NBC Bay Area also reached out to the Secretary of State’s Office, but have not yet heard back.

headlines.title: Paul Pelosi charged with misdemeanor hit-and-run over collision in Napa County
headlines.description: Paul Pelosi, husband of former House Speaker Nancy Pelosi, was charged Friday with a misdemeanor hit-and-run over a collision with a parked car. Pelosi, 86, was driving his brown convertible July 3 in Yountville, a town in the heart of wine country, when he struck a legally parked car on the side of the road, the Napa County Sheriff’s Office said in a statement at the time. He briefly stopped and then allegedly drove away. The parked car was unoccupied and no injuries were reported. Pelosi did not have any alcohol in his system, according to the statement. State law requires drivers involved in accidents resulting in property damage to stop and provide their vehicle and license information; Pelosi was charged with a misdemeanor violation of that law. In addition, Pelosi was charged with an infraction of making an unlawful turn, Napa County prosecutors said Friday. No attorney for Paul Pelosi was listed in court records. Nancy Pelosi’s press office did not immediately respond to a call seeking comment. Paul Pelosi’s court appearance is scheduled for Aug. 14. Pelosi pleaded guilty in 2022 to misdemeanor charges of driving under the influence in Napa County and was sentenced to five days in jail and three years of probation. He served two days in jail and received good conduct credit for two other days, leaving just one day to serve in a work program at the courthouse. As part of his probation, Pelosi was required to pay thousands in fines and victim restitution, attend a three-month drinking driver class and install an ignition interlock device, which forces drivers to provide a breath sample to prove sobriety before the engine will start. That same year he was attacked and severely beaten with a hammer at the couple’s San Francisco home.

headlines.title: Crews locate wreckage of boat that sank in San Francisco Bay
headlines.description: Crews on Friday located the wreckage of a boat that sank in San Francisco Bay earlier in the week, leaving two people dead and two others missing, police said. The 49-foot cabin cruiser named Volare was found by the San Francisco Police Department’s Marine Unit in the vicinity of where the boat sank Tuesday afternoon, according to police. The Marine Unit had been utilizing boat-mounted sonar to locate the vessel since it sank, police said. Crews have since turned to using a remotely operated vehicle to assess the wreckage. Police will work with partner agencies to evaluate recovery options and determine if it’s safe and feasible to recover the wreckage, police said. San Francisco Jul 16 San Francisco Jul 16 The boat sank with 20 people aboard after being hit by a wave and capsizing, officials said. The group was on the boat to scatter the ashes of a loved one. One man, Clifford Boisa, died immediately after being retrieved from the chilly water, and three people were listed as missing. On Thursday, police recovered the body of one of the missing people, identified as Tondra Madruga, 58, also known as Tondra Miller. Ralph Boisa said his extended family and some close friends were on the boat Tuesday to celebrate the life of his daughter, who died over a decade ago. Madruga was a friend. The two people who remain missing are Ralph Boisa’s sister, Carol, and Clifford Boisa’s wife, Jackie, he said.
headlines.source: NBC Bay Area staff and The Associated Press

headlines.title: YMCA summer camp counselor accused of sexually assaulting 2nd girl in Morgan Hill
headlines.description: Police said Friday the YMCA summer camp counselor accused of sexually assaulting a girl at a summer day camp in Morgan Hill earlier this month had allegedly done the same thing to a second girl that same day. Morgan Hill police said the report came on July 9, the same day 25-year-old Anthony Joseph Ramos of Gilroy, a YMCA summer camp counselor, was arrested after allegedly sexually assaulting a 10-year-old girl. According to authorities, the second incident occurred while the 10-year-old victim was attending the YMCA summer day camp on a field trip to the Morgan Hill Aquatic Center on the same day as the first victim. Officials said Ramon has been working with YMCA since Aug. 6, 2024. After the first incident, authorities said Ramon was immediately placed on leave. In light of the new report, Ramos was arrested on suspicion of lewd and lascivious acts on a minor on Thursday, according to police. Authorities said he has since posted bail and was released from custody.

headlines.title: Brush fire burns roughly 50 acres near Clayton
headlines.description: A brush fire was burning near Clayton Friday afternoon. The blaze, which broke out near Morgan Territory and Marsh Creek roads, has scorched roughly 50 acres and was burning with a moderate rate of spread, according to Cal Fire. Authorities added the fire is 10% contained. The fire began shortly before 3 p.m., and Contra Costa Fire officials said it moved quickly, with the terrain making it difficult for crews to access. “First units that arrived found a rapidly moving fire moving uphill in somewhat difficult conditions,” Contra Costa County Fire Chief Aaron McAlister said. It prompted an evacuation warning to Morgan Territory State Park and Mount Diablo State Park. Authorities warned residents in the area they should prepare to evacuate. Morgan Territory Road was temporarily closed before being reopened Friday evening. For residents like Juan Rios, who says this area has a history of brush fires, any evacuation warning is enough to get his attention. “We get pretty concerned because we’re surrounded with pine trees, our houses can be burned down in a heartbeat,” he said. Firefighters expect to be out here overnight, working to build containment lines and keep the fire from spreading. This is a developing story. Details may change as more information becomes available. Stay tuned for updates. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Lithium-ion batteries suspected in recent fires inside garbage trucks in San Jose
headlines.description: Lithium-ion batteries are believed to be responsible for a series of recent fires inside garbage trucks in San Jose. The San Jose Fire Department said there have been four garbage truck fires in the last month alone, with the latest occurring on Friday. “We know when lithium batteries are crushed or damaged, they can release sparks which can ignite materials around them, which then when they’re placed in the collection trucks can ignite the materials and cause fires,” Integrated Waste Management Deputy Director Judy Erlandson said. When a fire breaks out in a garbage truck, the driver has to dump the debris onto the street so firefighters can extinguish the blaze. San Jose leaders said no battery of any kind should ever go into a garbage or recycling bin. The Santa Clara County Household Hazardous Waste Program safely recycles lithium-ion batteries for residents who make an appointment.

headlines.title: Driver was going over 140 mph before double-fatal crash in San Jose, DA says
headlines.description: A 31-year-old San Jose man was speeding in excess of 140 mph in the moments leading up to a fiery crash late last year that left a mother and her 2-year-old child dead, the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office announced Friday. Zachary Chernicky was arrested on Monday and later charged with two counts of murder in connection with the Dec. 2, 2025, crash on northbound Highway 87 near Curtner Avenue that claimed the lives of 30-year-old Ivana Balistreri and her daughter Lilliana. The district attorney’s office said Chernicky was driving his Tesla 142 mph 15 seconds before the crash, 126 mph five seconds before impact and 102 mph when he plowed into the back of a stopped Lexus carrying Ivana and Lilliana. Chernicky’s Tesla, the Lexus and another Tesla burst into flames. Ivana and Lilliana burned to death, according to the district attorney’s office. Chernicky suffered minor injuries. “The law says that it is murder when someone shoots into a crowd,” District Attorney Jeff Rosen said in a statement. “When someone is driving 140 mph, then the driver is the shooter and their car is the bullet.” Chernicky was arraigned on Tuesday and remanded to jail without bail, the district attorney’s office said. His next court date was set for Aug. 25. San Jose Jul 16 San Jose Dec 4, 2025 San Jose Dec 2, 2025 Adult, child killed in fiery crash on Highway 87 in San Jose

headlines.title: BART records highest ridership month since pandemic-induced low
headlines.description: BART trains are filling up again and people are excited about it. Officials at BART announced Thursday that June was the transit agency’s best month for ridership since the COVID-19 pandemic-induced slowdown cratered passenger numbers, and BART credited major sporting and community events with the dramatic rise. Ridership rose by 18.6% in June as compared to the same period last year. The month marked the end of the agency’s 2025-2026 fiscal year, which outperformed the previous year by 13%. BART credits the steady rise in ridership recovery, now at about 56% of 2019 levels, to the agency’s “growing role beyond the traditional commute.” People have been regularly using trains to travel to major sporting events, such as the 2026 FIFA World Cup, and large cultural celebrations like San Francisco Pride. Several post-pandemic records were also broken. June saw the highest single ridership day, the highest average weekday and weekend ridership, and all-time highs for certain stations. Chris Filippi, a spokesperson with BART, said the positive trend is why the agency has decided to add capacity during peak commute hours by bringing back 10-car trains — which had not been regularly used since the start of the pandemic. “It’s very exciting to see not just the increase in June but what is now two consecutive years of double-digit ridership growth,” said Filippi. While ridership is showing positive signs of recovery, the transit agency warned fare revenue remains insufficient to sustain regular BART operations. Since the depletion of pandemic-related emergency federal funds, the agency and others continue to face a budget deficit unless new sources of revenue are found. The Connect Bay Area Act, a regional measure proposing a sales tax in five Bay Area counties, received enough signatures to appear on the ballot in this year’s November election. The measure will introduce a sales tax if approved by voters in Alameda, Contra Costa, San Francisco, San Mateo, and Santa Clara counties. Proponents have said it will help create a robust new source of revenue that will help reduce the likelihood of drastic cuts to service and operations.
headlines.source: Andres Jimenez Larios | Bay City News

headlines.title: OUSD expands partnership with U.S. Soccer to grow game at Oakland schools
headlines.description: Oakland Unified School District announced Thursday that it will expand its partnership with the U.S. Soccer Federation to grow youth soccer opportunities at Oakland schools. The district will work specifically with the Soccer Forward Foundation, U.S. Soccer’s community-impact effort that was launched to expand nationwide access with connection to the FIFA World Cup 26. The partnership aims to expand the Soccer at Schools initiative, which will train educators across the district on how to integrate soccer into the school day starting in the 2026-27 school year. Soccer at Schools is a nationwide U.S. Soccer program launched in March, to grow the game by bringing soccer opportunities, such as physical education lessons, educator training and equipment, to every school nationwide by 2030. “Soccer has the power to create healthier schools, stronger communities and greater opportunities for young people,” Lex Chalat, executive director of the Soccer Forward Foundation, said in a statement. The district hopes the program will give its educators the “tools, resources and confidence” to expand soccer opportunities at Oakland schools. The program will also work to connect OUSD students with community-based soccer programming throughout the Bay Area. The program builds on the district’s recent efforts to expand soccer opportunities at its schools. In January 2025, OUSD reached an agreement with Soccer Without Borders, a nonprofit organization that uses soccer to help underprivileged youth, to provide year-round after-school soccer programming and academic tutoring for middle and high school students at select schools in the district. In April, an official FIFA Arena, a mini-sized FIFA-sponsored soccer pitch, was opened at Montera Middle School in Oakland’s Montclair neighborhood in partnership with U.S. Soccer and other community partners. In a statement, OUSD Superintendent Denise Saddler said that through Soccer at Schools, OUSD is working alongside partners to “enhance the educational experiences of Oakland students.” “We know soccer will help an increasing number of students as they learn and grow as athletes, people and leaders,” Saddler said. In Oakland, the initiative is delivered through the Oakland Athletic League, OUSD’s official interscholastic athletics league, and in direct collaboration with Oakland Genesis, an Oakland-based nonprofit that provides youth soccer programming. Since 2019, Oakland Genesis has provided after-school soccer programming to OUSD schools. The program is also supported by many Bay Area sports-focused community organizations like the San Jose Earthquakes, Bay FC, Bay Area Host Committee and other major Bay Area youth soccer teams and leagues. “By bringing together schools, community organizations and local leaders, we’re helping ensure the momentum created by the tournament results in meaningful experiences for Oakland’s young people long after the final match is played,” said Zaileen Janmohammed, president and chief executive officer of the Bay Area Host Committee, the organization which helped coordinate the World Cup and Super Bowl in the region. The host committee recently announced its plans to become a permanent leadership commission that will continue to bring sports events to the Bay Area for years to come, with the grassroots growth of sports culture in the Bay Area remaining an organizational priority.

headlines.title: SFDA charges man who allegedly hit elderly pedestrian while fleeing police
headlines.description: A 35-year-old man who allegedly hit an elderly pedestrian on the sidewalk near Union Square while fleeing police earlier this week is facing several charges, including assault with a deadly weapon and elder abuse, San Francisco District Attorney Brooke Jenkins said Thursday. Melthon Branch was first spotted by police about 8:30 p.m. Monday while he was riding the scooter on the north sidewalk of Ellis Street towards Jones Street. The officer recognized Branch and knew he had an outstanding warrant from the U.S. Marshals Service. When the officer tried to pull him over, however, Branch allegedly fled, riding his scooter on the sidewalk where pedestrians were walking and into busy traffic on the road, according to the district attorney. He allegedly drove the wrong way for several blocks, violating multiple traffic signals along the way. Pedestrians had to move to avoid being struck as he weaved in and out of the street and sidewalks, the district attorney said. The suspect was riding north on the west sidewalk of Powell approaching O’Farrell Street when he allegedly struck a 72-year-old woman, throwing her to the ground. The woman was hospitalized with a fractured elbow and a broken wrist that required surgery, according to the district attorney. After the collision, Branch allegedly ran towards the 200 block of O’Farrell Street and dove under a table where restaurant customers were eating. He was taken into custody there, and officers allegedly seized suspected methamphetamine, over $2,000 in cash, narcotics paraphernalia, a digital scale, unused packaging including Ziploc baggies, and three credit cards with other people’s names. Branch was charged with several crimes, including leaving the scene of an accident with an allegation that he personally inflicted great bodily injury on the victim, elder abuse with an allegation that he personally inflicted great bodily injury on the victim, and that he personally used a deadly weapon. He is also charged with assault with a deadly weapon, battery with serious bodily injury, and possession for sale of methamphetamine.

headlines.title: San Jose's San Pedro Square prepares for final World Cup watch parties
headlines.description: San Jose’s San Pedro Square is getting ready for one final weekend of FIFA World Cup watch parties after weeks of drawing large crowds. Businesses in the area are expecting another busy weekend as fans gather to watch the tournament’s final two matches. The third-place match between France and England is scheduled for Saturday, followed by the World Cup final between Spain and Argentina on Sunday. At the Old Wagon Saloon, employees say fans have packed the venue throughout the tournament and expect another strong turnout. “We think Saturday is going to be grande, it’s going to be big,” said Sonny Walker of the Old Wagon Saloon. “I think that those two matches Saturday and Sunday are going to be really big, doing all the preparations, tightening down the tables, those chairs, all that little stuff.” Walker said some staff members hope the excitement surrounding the World Cup continues even after Sunday’s championship match, while others are looking forward to a chance to catch their breath after weeks of hosting large crowds. San Jose Jul 8 San Jose Jul 1 Security increases as World Cup watch parties bring thousands to downtown San Jose
